dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 responding for chest pain. Patient is a 52-year-old female at a doctor's office in [CITY].
Hi, my name is Ava, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I was here for my blood sugar when I suddenly started having chest pain.
Can you describe the chest pain for me? When did it start, and was it sudden or slow to develop?
It was sudden. It started about 30 minutes ago. It's on my left side and radiates to my shoulder.
Can you tell me your name, today's date, and where we are right now?
My name is Julia, today is December 2nd and we're in the doctor's office in [CITY].
Did you say [CITY] specifically? Can you look up to the right with me? And back down slowly.
Right side, left side. Yes, I can follow your hand.
Great. Can you tell me where you are on a scale from 0 to 10 in terms of how bad the pain is right now?
About 4. It's sharp-like a pin. It doesn't go away.
Got it. And if you nod your head up and then down for me, that would be helpful.
Of course.
Your airway is patent, and you're self-maintaining. That's good. You'll need to stay in this position for now for comfort.
We noticed you're having some mild breathing difficulty when lying flat. How are you feeling now that you're sitting?
Much better, thank you.
We're going to give you some oxygen to see if it helps.
Ma'am, we are going to put on 2LPM oxygen via nasal cannula. It may tickle a little.
Would you mind it if we took a look at your blood pressure and pulse for us?
Blood pressure is 183 over 90, and heart rate is 74.
Respirations are 18. Skin is warm and dry.
SpO2 at 99 percent.
Pulse is 74-regular.
Thanks for staying with us, Julia. We're hearing clear bilateral breath sounds and a clear waveform.
Let's now do a few quick tests so we can understand what's going on.
Blood sugar is 295 mg/dL. That's a bit high, but it's consistent with your diabetes.
We also want to check if you have any numbness or weakness in your arms or legs, or difficulty talking.
No, I feel fine. No numbness or weakness.
Great. Your pupils look normal, equal, and reactive. That's reassuring.
You mentioned that the pain came on suddenly while you were in the office. What were you doing before it started?
I was just sitting there, waiting for my blood sugar to be drawn.
I see. Any nausea, dizziness, or shortness of breath before or with the pain?
Just some tightness in the chest. No nausea. Abdomen doesn't hurt.
Anything unusual prior to the pain? Like eating a heavy meal or physical exertion?
No. I was just resting. No food.
Can you tell me what medications you're currently taking?
I take insulin and Lisinopril.
Our awareness is that you were already given Aspirin and 0.4mg Nitro by the office staff.
Do you have any pre-existing conditions? For example, diabetes or high blood pressure?
Yes. I have diabetes, hypertension, and had a heart attack in 2013.
And do you have a history of any other chronic conditions such as heart failure or aortic aneurysm?
I have CHF and an AAA diagnosed in 2012.
How long ago did you last eat or drink anything?
Maybe 3 hours ago.
What exactly did you do when you noticed the onset of the chest pain?
I was just waiting for my glucose test. I wasn't doing anything else.
And currently, using the same 0 to 10 scale, how bad is the chest pain now?
Around a 4, maybe a bit less now.
You mentioned it started in your upper left chest and radiates to your shoulder. Is the radiation still there?
Yes, but not as bad. Still up into my shoulder.
Do you have any difficulty moving or feeling in your arms, legs, or anywhere else?
No. I feel fine. I can move everything.
That's good news. Your pupils are equal and reactive-no signs of neurological compromise.
We are performing a 4-lead EKG now as part of the cardiac protocol.
The rhythm is showing sinus rhythm. No acute changes at this time.
Now placing a 12-lead EKG to get more detailed cardiac data.
The 12-lead EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm with incomplete right bundle branch block and some T wave changes-no signs of acute MI.
No acute respiratory distress-bilateral breath sounds are clear and equal.
I'll do a skin and mucous membrane inspection real quick.
Skin is dry, warm, with no rashes, bruises, or other abnormalities.
IV attempt will be held in abeyance as the patient is currently stable.
Protocol 2-1, suspected cardiac chest pain, activated.
BP is now 197 over 84. We note a drop in SpO2 to 90%.
Heart rate is 69 and regular. Respirations are 16 now.
How is the chest pain now? Is it stable, worse, or better?
Still there. Same as before. It's 4 out of 10.
Any movement or position change makes it better or worse?
No, it stays the same.
We're going to ready you for transport now. We're maintaining you in semi-Fowler's position for comfort.
Cot is secured with straps and side rails. Patient is stable for transport.
The hospital-[COMPANY_NAME]-will be the destination as requested.
Yes, I'd rather go there. They have my medical records.
You mentioned you have an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Has that affected you in any way recently?
No. It was diagnosed in 2012, but it hasn't flared up since.
The blood glucose level was 295 mg/dL previously. I don't need your current level given your clinical status.
Okay, your chest pain has stayed constant-still 4 out of 10. Any other areas hurting besides the chest?
No, just the chest and a little down the arm.
Recalling the administration of 2LPM oxygen. Currently tickling but tolerated well by the patient.
Repeat respiratory assessment: clear bilateral breath sounds consistent with initial exam.
Anything else developing since the onset you haven't mentioned yet?
No. The pain feels the same. No other problems.
Care will be continuing en route to [COMPANY_NAME].
Copies of your medical records and provider notes will be sent to the facility in advance.
Got it. And the pain scale remains 4/10. Are you expecting it to change at all during transport?
Not much, just will stay the same.
Reality check: we've double-checked straps, side rails, and the cot position before rolling out.
Everything set for transport as per policy.
Final check of airway confirmation in semi-Fowler's position.
No signs of airway compromise. Fully breathable.
Respiratory re-check-16, regular, patent patency maintained.
One more time-no signs of acute respiratory distress. Breathing effort is normal.
And to recap, you were getting your BGL drawn. Any changes in diet or activity since then?
No. I had a sandwich at 10, but that's it.
Understand. Your management team at the hospital is aware of the procedures and meds we documented.
If rhythm changes or pain intensifies, we'll adjust en route.
[COMPANY_NAME] will be handling your care. Any surgical center or preference ahead not needed at this point.
Ambulance securing process is complete. Doors closed.
En route in 4-wheel drive at 35 mph.
Final verbal check-Julia, feeling steady or okay?
Yep. Everything seems good.
